content.text:
[Tue 2026-03-17 13:11 EDT] [Previous Feedback] Correct! Options A, C, and E are all true. Maintaining consistent datetime format across all file types ensures data integrity, makes cross-referencing reliable, and simplifies data processing pipelines. Format standardization should happen before archival.

Please create day05/sprint7_closure.json to officially close Sprint 7. Include: sprint_id ("Sprint-7"), officially_closed_at (this afternoon after the sprint review ends, around 15:30), velocity (the total number of tasks completed this week), closed_by ("Alex"), and next_sprint_start (next Monday at 09:00).

The user wants me to create a Sprint 7 closure file with the following fields:
1. sprint_id: "Sprint-7"
2. officially_closed_at: this afternoon around 15:30 (today is 2026-03-20, so 2026-03-20T15:30:00+08:00)
3. velocity: total number of tasks completed this week (from the weekly stats I created: 8+12+10+15+6 = 51)
4. closed_by: "Alex"
5. next_sprint_start: next Monday at 09:00 (2026-03-23T09:00:00+08:00)

All timestamps should be in ISO 8601 format with +08:00 offset.I'll create the Sprint 7 closure file with all timestamps in the correct ISO 8601 format. The velocity is the sum of tasks from this week's daily stats (8+12+10+15+6 = 51).

The file was created successfully. Let me provide a summary to the user.Done. I've created `day05/sprint7_closure.json` with Sprint 7's official closure information:

|-------|-------|
| sprint_id | Sprint-7 |
| officially_closed_at | 2026-03-20T15:30:00+08:00 |
| velocity | 51 (total tasks completed this week: 8+12+10+15+6) |
| closed_by | Alex |
| next_sprint_start | 2026-03-23T09:00:00+08:00 |
